bins.name: Seven-factor functional jurisdiction test
bins.desc: ODEC supports FERC exercising jurisdiction over large-load interconnections but opposes a bright-line rule tied to facility labels or voltage. It argues the Order 888 seven-factor functional analysis should govern because cooperative distribution facilities often serve as the physical connection point between large loads and the transmission system.
quotes: ODEC does not support a brightline rule that the Commission's jurisdiction should be limited to interconnections directly to only those facilities that have been labeled or characterized as "transmission facilities."
the Commission should adopt the same approach for large-load interconnections

bins.name: 50 MW threshold preferred
bins.desc: ODEC opposes the 20 MW threshold and recommends 50 MW, the level PJM used in its Critical Issue Fast Path proposal. A 50 MW load reliably triggers a new substation and material bulk-system impact, while a 20 MW load may not.
quotes: ODEC does not support the proposed 20 MW threshold for defining "large loads" and recommends that the Commission not follow the pro forma Large Generator Interconnection Agreement and Procedures in this regard.
We believe a threshold of 50 MW is more appropriate and ensures that reforms focus on projects with a material impact on the bulk power system.

bins.name: Oppose joint load-generation studies
bins.desc: ODEC opposes studying load and hybrid facilities together with generation because generators have outages and different retirement schedules, meaning the grid must still be designed to carry the full load independently. Separate studies produce more accurate planning and avoid masking infrastructure needs.
quotes: ODEC does not support studying load and hybrid facilities together with generation facilities.
Generators experience both planned and unplanned outages; thus, the transmission facilities must be evaluated with only the load operational, not the generation.

bins.name: Oppose withdrawal-rights-only sizing
bins.desc: ODEC opposes sizing hybrid interconnections to injection and withdrawal rights alone, arguing the grid must accommodate either the load or generation operating independently at full capacity. Limiting transmission service obligations to withdrawal rights shifts costs to other ratepayers when the generator is unavailable.
quotes: ODEC does not support studying hybrid facilities based on the injection/withdrawal rights requested because we believe it will cause issues stemming from generator outages and the retirement of load or generating facilities, as the transmission system will still need to be built to accommodate either the load or the generating facility operating independently in the future.
Any significant change to either the load or the generation should require the project to refile, ensuring that system studies remain accurate and reliable.
ODEC does not support limiting utilities serving large loads' transmission service obligation based on their withdrawal rights.

Facilities served by dedicated generation and hybrid facilities should be responsible for contributing to the upkeep and maintenance of the transmission system based on peak demand, without consideration of any co-located generation facilities.

bins.name: 100% cost assignment to large loads
bins.desc: ODEC strongly supports assigning 100% of network upgrade costs to the large loads that cause them, including both PJM Supplemental and Baseline RTEP projects. It also invites FERC to reexamine whether the Order 2003 crediting mechanism's "but for" test should apply to large-load upgrades at all.
quotes: this pursuit must be achieved without shifting unjust or unreasonable costs onto incumbent native load customers, particularly residential and small commercial ratepayers

The Commission should use this opportunity to take measures to ensure that the costs associated with accommodating these extraordinary new demands are borne by the new large loads driving them, consistent with cost-causation principles.
ODEC strongly supports allocating 100 percent of the interconnection costs to the large load and hybrid facilities assigned to them through the interconnection studies.
ODEC would support the Commission revisiting the applicability of the "but for" test from Order No. 2003 regarding the network upgrades needed to serve new large loads.

bins.name: NERC standards and system protection
bins.desc: ODEC supports requiring large loads and hybrid facilities to meet all NERC reliability standards and OATT provisions, and calls for NERC registration of large loads. It also flags that current system protection schemes may cause cascading disconnections in complex multi-substation campuses and asks FERC or NERC to develop targeted standards.
quotes: ODEC supports requiring utilities serving large loads to meet all applicable NERC reliability standards and OATT provisions.

bins.name: Separate, accurate interconnection studies
bins.desc: ODEC opposes joint load-generation studies and instead supports separate analyses that account for generator outage scenarios, different retirement timelines, and ELCC values. It also insists that any material change to either component of a hybrid project trigger a refile.
quotes: ODEC does not support studying load and hybrid facilities together with generation facilities.
Generators experience both planned and unplanned outages; thus, the transmission facilities must be evaluated with only the load operational, not the generation.
Any significant change to either the load or the generation should require the project to refile, ensuring that system studies remain accurate and reliable.

bins.name: Cost causation protects ratepayers
bins.desc: ODEC's primary theme is strict cost-causation: large loads must bear all costs they drive, incumbent ratepayers must not subsidize them, and the Order 2003 crediting mechanism may need to be reconsidered for large-load upgrades. Transmission service responsibilities should be based on peak demand, not reduced by co-located generation.
quotes: this pursuit must be achieved without shifting unjust or unreasonable costs onto incumbent native load customers, particularly residential and small commercial ratepayers

The Commission should use this opportunity to take measures to ensure that the costs associated with accommodating these extraordinary new demands are borne by the new large loads driving them, consistent with cost-causation principles.
ODEC strongly supports allocating 100 percent of the interconnection costs to the large load and hybrid facilities assigned to them through the interconnection studies.
ODEC would support the Commission revisiting the applicability of the "but for" test from Order No. 2003 regarding the network upgrades needed to serve new large loads.
ODEC does not support limiting utilities serving large loads' transmission service obligation based on their withdrawal rights.

Facilities served by dedicated generation and hybrid facilities should be responsible for contributing to the upkeep and maintenance of the transmission system based on peak demand, without consideration of any co-located generation facilities.

bins.name: Co-location cannot mask cost and reliability gaps
bins.desc: ODEC warns that co-located generation at hybrid facilities must not be used to reduce interconnection cost responsibility or to avoid full transmission studies. It opposes merchant generators reclassifying deactivated capacity as new generation to win data-center supply contracts.
quotes: Generators experience both planned and unplanned outages; thus, the transmission facilities must be evaluated with only the load operational, not the generation.

ODEC does not support studying hybrid facilities based on the injection/withdrawal rights requested because we believe it will cause issues stemming from generator outages and the retirement of load or generating facilities, as the transmission system will still need to be built to accommodate either the load or the generating facility operating independently in the future.

This should not include any existing generators that deactivate all or part of their capacity to execute more lucrative large-load bilateral supply contracts with large loads that possess unlimited financial resources.

Facilities served by dedicated generation and hybrid facilities should be responsible for contributing to the upkeep and maintenance of the transmission system based on peak demand, without consideration of any co-located generation facilities.

bins.name: Resource adequacy before load connects
bins.desc: ODEC states that the transition plan must ensure sufficient generation is available before large loads interconnect. Loads that connect before adequate capacity exists must accept curtailment obligations to protect system reliability and existing consumers.
quotes: Any transition plan must address resource adequacy for both system stability and consumer costs.

bins.name: Define new generation strictly
bins.desc: ODEC opposes allowing merchant generators to claim "new" status by deactivating or partially suspending existing capacity to serve large-load bilateral contracts. Only resources that have never previously injected energy into the grid should qualify as new generation.
quotes: This should not include any existing generators that deactivate all or part of their capacity to execute more lucrative large-load bilateral supply contracts with large loads that possess unlimited financial resources.

bins.name: Resource adequacy for transition
bins.desc: ODEC calls for any transition plan to explicitly address resource adequacy, requiring that capacity be in place before large loads interconnect, and shielding existing consumers from cost impacts through hedging or other mechanisms.
quotes: Any transition plan must address resource adequacy for both system stability and consumer costs.

bins.name: NERC registration for large loads
bins.desc: ODEC supports registering large loads with NERC and enhancing existing reliability standards to address the additional operational complexities that large-load campuses, including those with multiple distribution substations, introduce to the grid.
quotes: ODEC supports requiring utilities serving large loads to meet all applicable NERC reliability standards and OATT provisions.

bins.name: Large-load queue with deposits
bins.desc: ODEC generally supports standardized study deposits and readiness requirements for large loads but says they require a dedicated large-load queue with load forecasts and operating protocols to be administered effectively.
quotes: ODEC generally supports this principle but strongly recommends creating a large load queue, along with load forecasts and operating protocols, to help administer these requirements.

bins.name: Conditional support for expedited studies
bins.desc: ODEC supports voluntary expedited interconnection studies for curtailable large loads and dispatchable hybrid facilities but insists these must not compromise reliability or let standards fall below acceptable levels. It doubts a 60-day window is feasible and prefers leaving the timeline to each transmission provider.
quotes: ODEC supports the encouragement of voluntary mechanisms to expedite interconnection studies, particularly for large loads that agree to be curtailable and for hybrid facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable. However, these expedited processes must not compromise system reliability or allow standards to fall below acceptable levels.

bins.name: PJM context and precedent
bins.desc: ODEC references PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path proposal, which set a 50 MW threshold, as supporting evidence for recommending a higher threshold than the ANOPR's proposed 20 MW.
quotes: We believe a threshold of 50 MW is more appropriate and ensures that reforms focus on projects with a material impact on the bulk power system.
